item.title: Cracks are starting to form on fusion energy’s funding boom
item.dc:creator: Tim De Chant
item.description: Fusion startups and investors risk deepening fissures if some key disagreements aren't settled amicably.

item.title: Blue Origin successfully re-uses a New Glenn rocket for the first time ever
item.dc:creator: Sean O'Kane
item.description: It's a major milestone for the company's new mega-rocket system, putting it in position to challenge SpaceX's dominance of the global launch market.

item.title: Tesla brings its robotaxi service to Dallas and Houston
item.dc:creator: Anthony Ha
item.description: The company now offers robotaxi service in three cities, all of them in Texas, after launching in Austin last year and starting to offer rides without safety drivers in January 2026.

item.title: VC Ron Conway says he has a ‘rare form of cancer’
item.dc:creator: Anthony Ha
item.description: Conway wrote that he “will be stepping back from some of my usual activities,” but he will “continue to support” founders backed by his firm SV Angel.

item.title: AI chip startup Cerebras files for IPO
item.dc:creator: Anthony Ha
item.description: In recent months, the company announced an agreement with Amazon Web Services to use Cerebras chips in Amazon data centers, as well as a deal with OpenAI reportedly worth more than $10 billion.

item.title: Anthropic’s relationship with the Trump administration seems to be thawing
item.dc:creator: Anthony Ha
item.description: Despite recently being designated a supply-chain risk by the Pentagon, Anthropic is still talking to high-level members of the Trump administration.

item.title: The App Store is booming again, and AI may be why
item.dc:creator: Sarah Perez
item.description: New data from Appfigures shows a swell of new app launches in 2026, suggesting AI tools could be fueling a mobile software boom.

item.title: Once close enough for an acquisition, Stripe and Airwallex are now going after each other
item.dc:creator: Connie Loizos
item.description: For most of its life, Airwallex and Stripe have mostly operated in different geographies, selling to different buyers. That's changing.

item.title: Sam Altman’s project World looks to scale its human verification empire. First stop: Tinder.
item.dc:creator: Lucas Ropek
item.description: World, which has raised eyebrows (but also a lot of interest) with its Orb-centered anonymous verification project, is looking to expand its influence via a bevy of new partnerships.

item.title: Kevin Weil and Bill Peebles exit OpenAI as company continues to shed ‘side quests’
item.dc:creator: Rebecca Bellan
item.description: Kevin Weil and Bill Peebles are leaving OpenAI as the company shuts down Sora and folds its science team, signaling a sharp pivot away from consumer moonshots toward enterprise AI.

item.title: Man who hacked US Supreme Court filing system sentenced to probation
item.dc:creator: Lorenzo Franceschi-Bicchierai
item.description: Nicholas Moore hacked into three U.S. government networks using stolen credentials, and then bragged about it and posted victims' personal data on Instagram under the handle @ihackedthegovernment.

item.title: Sources: Cursor in talks to raise $2B+ at $50B valuation as enterprise growth surges
item.dc:creator: Marina Temkin
item.description: Returning backers a16z and Thrive are expected to lead the round.

item.title: ‘Tokenmaxxing’ is making developers less productive than they think
item.dc:creator: Tim Fernholz
item.description: There's a lot more code — but it's a lot more expensive and requires a lot more rewriting.

item.title: Hackers are abusing unpatched Windows security flaws to hack into organizations
item.dc:creator: Lorenzo Franceschi-Bicchierai
item.description: A security researcher published details of three security vulnerabilities in Windows Defender, and the code used to exploit them. Now, hackers are taking advantage of the vulnerabilities in real-life attacks, according to a cybersecurity firm.

item.title: Zoom teams up with World to verify humans in meetings
item.dc:creator: Ivan Mehta
item.description: Meeting platform Zoom has announced a partnership with World, Sam Altman's human ID verification company, to ensure that the people attending meetings are actually human and not AI-generated imposters. Zoom will show a badge on verified participants' tile.

item.title: Gigs turns your concert history into a personal live music archive
item.dc:creator: Sarah Perez
item.description: New iPhone app Gigs uses AI to turn old tickets, screenshots, and emails into a personal concert archive with stats, memories, and more.

item.title: Chef Robotics escaped the robot cooking graveyard and says it’s thriving — here’s why
item.dc:creator: Lucas Ropek
item.description: The company, which deploys AI-guided robot arms for food production, says it is looking to expand its services to provide for a broader array of customers.

item.title: Uber will now pick up your returns from your doorstep
item.dc:creator: Kirsten Korosec
item.description: The new returns feature is the company's latest effort to become an everything app. There are limitations to the new service, and there is, of course, a courier fee.

item.title: Anthropic launches Claude Design, a new product for creating quick visuals
item.dc:creator: Aisha Malik
item.description: The company says Claude Design is intended to help people like founders and product managers without a design background share their ideas more easily.

item.title: With US spy laws set to expire, lawmakers are split over protecting Americans from warrantless surveillance
item.dc:creator: Zack Whittaker
item.description: Some lawmakers are calling for widespread reforms following years of surveillance scandals and abuses across successive U.S. administrations. But even if the spy law known as Section 702 expires in April, the government's spy powers will not automatically lapse.
